stem: In the cyclic group \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \) (integers modulo [[nval]] under addition), how many elements have order exactly [[dval]]? (Note that [[dval]] divides [[nval]].)
solution: A cyclic group of order \( n \) has exactly \( \varphi(d) \) elements of order \( d \) for each divisor \( d \mid n \). Here \( d = [[dval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \), so \( \varphi(d) = p^{a-1}(p-1) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives the size of the order-\( [[dval]] \) subgroup, not the number of elements of order exactly \( [[dval]] \).
This counts the non-identity elements of that subgroup, which overcounts unless \( [[dval]] \) is prime.
This uses \( p-1 \) only, dropping the \( p^{a-1} \) factor of \( \varphi(p^{a}) \).
This is off by one from \( \varphi([[dval]]) \).
This is off by one from \( \varphi([[dval]]) \).
This is off by two from \( \varphi([[dval]]) \).

stem: In the cyclic group \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \), how many elements have order exactly \( d = [[dval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \)? (Note that \( d \) divides [[nval]].)
solution: Euler's totient is multiplicative on coprime factors: \( \varphi(d) = \varphi(p^{a})\varphi(q^{b}) = p^{a-1}(p-1)\,q^{b-1}(q-1) = [[answer]] \). A cyclic group of order \( n \) has exactly \( \varphi(d) \) elements of order \( d \) for a divisor \( d \mid n \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the prime-power factors \( p^{a-1} \) and \( q^{b-1} \).
This keeps only \( p^{a-1}q^{b-1} \) and forgets the \( (p-1)(q-1) \) factors.
This counts every non-identity element of the order-\( d \) subgroup, which overcounts.
This reports \( d \) itself, not \( \varphi(d) \).
This is off by one from \( \varphi(d) \).
This is off by one from \( \varphi(d) \).

stem: How many \( n \)-cycles are there in the symmetric group \( S_{[[n]]} \), i.e., permutations of \( \{1, 2, \dots, [[n]]\} \) that cyclically permute all [[n]] symbols in a single cycle? (Every such permutation has order exactly [[n]].)
solution: There are \( n! \) orderings of \( n \) symbols, and each \( n \)-cycle can be written starting from any of its \( n \) symbols, so the count is \( \frac{n!}{n} = (n-1)! = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts ALL permutations of \( S_{[[n]]} \), not just the full cycles.
This is the order of each such element, not the number of them.
This forgets that \( n! \) must already be divided by \( n \) (double counts the rotation).
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This multiplies back by \( n \), undoing the division that fixes the starting point.

stem: How many transpositions (permutations that swap exactly two symbols and fix all others) are there in the symmetric group \( S_{[[n]]} \)? (Each transposition has order 2.)
solution: A transposition is determined by an unordered pair of symbols to swap, so the count is \( \binom{[[n]]}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports \( n \), not the number of pairs of symbols.
This counts all permutations of \( S_{[[n]]} \), not just transpositions.
This counts ORDERED pairs, double-counting each transposition.
This is off by one from \( \binom{n}{2} \).
This is off by one from \( \binom{n}{2} \).
This doubles the correct count by treating each pair as ordered.

stem: The dihedral group \( D_{[[n]]} \) of symmetries of a regular [[n]]-gon has order [[gorder]] ([[n]] rotations and [[n]] reflections), where [[n]] is odd. Since [[n]] is odd, no nontrivial rotation has order 2. How many elements of \( D_{[[n]]} \) have order exactly 2?
solution: Every reflection in \( D_{[[n]]} \) has order 2, giving [[n]] such elements; since [[n]] is odd, the rotation subgroup (cyclic of order [[n]]) has no element of order 2. So the total is [[answer]].
distractor_rationales: This counts every non-identity element of \( D_{[[n]]} \), not just those of order 2.
This mistakenly adds the identity to the reflection count.
This undercounts the reflections by one.
This double counts the reflections.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This overcounts the reflections by two.
